commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=b6614e6534f7f68935138af91492a284b247a001)
commands.review_summary:
The PR wires configured Gateway SSH transport into connection classification, managed tunnel startup, loopback URL rewriting, cleanup ownership, and related CLI/status/health/TUI/MCP/ACP caller coverage.

PR surface: Source +423, Tests +1489. Total +1912 across 43 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-reproducible: current main validates the configured remote URL before normal managed SSH client bootstrap can start a tunnel. I did not run a live repro in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining concrete blocker is a narrow lint failure in a changed test mock that automation can repair without choosing SSH transport product direction.

target.title: fix: sSH transport security check rejects connection before tunnel is established
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix classification: Root-cause fix for configured remote Gateway SSH transport setup, URL safety, and managed tunnel lifecycle.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High. The branch has a live configured-SSH Gateway proof using a real foreground OpenClaw Gateway plus an isolated local OpenSSH daemon, focused regression coverage for Gateway call/bootstrap/status/logs/TUI/TTS/security-audit/doctor/devices/Crestodian paths, SSH launcher failures, status diagnostics, default SSH port handling, core/test-src typechecks, `git diff --check`, and a local ClawSweeper commit review with no actionable code finding.
- Root cause: The source-of-truth invariant for configured SSH remotes was split across URL validation, tunnel bootstrap, and status probing. Config-derived SSH remote URLs were treated as final plaintext `ws://` targets before the managed SSH tunnel could rewrite them to loopback, and some status paths reused the local tunnel port as the remote SSH destination port.
- Why this is root-cause fix: This fixes the source invariant rather than masking the symptom: config-derived SSH remotes that own tunnel startup are classified before plaintext validation, every owning client/probe path receives the loopback runtime URL after startup, and local listener ports are separated from remote Gateway destination ports. CLI/env overrides plus explicit `transport: "direct"` stay on the direct plaintext safety path.
- Reliability guardrails added: Owned SSH tunnels are closed on Gateway call/bootstrap failures, status/health/doctor probe completion or failure, and long-lived ACP/MCP/operator approval client shutdown. Status scans report SSH tunnel startup failures as an unreachable Gateway snapshot instead of aborting the whole status report.
- Compatibility guardrails added: Omitted `gateway.remote.transport` follows the documented SSH default when an SSH target is configured. Omitted `gateway.remote.remotePort` keeps the documented remote-side default `18789`; configured URL/`--port` values remain local listener preferences unless explicitly used as the status command override. SSH spawn failures now reject through tunnel startup instead of becoming unhandled child-process errors.
- Existing CLI coverage retained: `openclaw logs` stays on the normal Gateway RPC client path for configured SSH remotes instead of failing in local-only preflight.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: The canonical contract is `gateway.remote.transport` deciding direct vs managed SSH ownership, `gateway.remote.sshTarget` defining the managed tunnel target, `gateway.remote.remotePort` defining the remote Gateway destination port, and the configured Gateway URL/CLI `--port` defining the local listener preference. URL validation protects final direct network targets; SSH bootstrap owns transforming config-derived SSH targets into loopback runtime URLs.
- Scope boundary: The diff is limited to the configured Gateway SSH transport contract and the command/client surfaces that either start, diagnose, or hold a managed Gateway tunnel. ACP/MCP/operator approval files are touched only to release the tunnel that was handed to long-lived clients; provider routing, session storage, message delivery semantics, schemas, migrations, UI, and auth policy are not changed.
- Patch quality notes: Broad catches only suppress best-effort tunnel cleanup failures so they do not mask the primary startup/connect/status error already being returned. `null`/`undefined` default returns are fail-closed classification results for "no configured SSH tunnel owner" or "no SSH target" paths. Test `expect.any(...)` assertions cover framework-provided timing/object details while asserting the observable tunnel/auth/URL contract, and the timeout values changed in tests are command-budget inputs used to make probe behavior deterministic.
- Large diff / split check: This PR is larger than an ideal one-file fix because the broken invariant crossed validation, tunnel bootstrap, one-shot Gateway calls, long-lived clients, TUI chat, gateway-backed TTS, security audit, Crestodian overview, devices fallback, status/health/doctor probes, logs, ACP, MCP, and operator approval shutdown. Splitting would leave at least one user-visible caller with the old pre-tunnel rejection or leaked tunnel lifecycle; each touched surface is covered by focused regression tests.
- Related open PR / competition scan: daily-fix found the existing author PR #96134 for issue #26250 and switched to updating it instead of opening a duplicate. No separate competing implementation is being introduced here.
- What did NOT change: CLI/env Gateway URL overrides still use the existing plaintext safety check; explicit `transport: "direct"` remains direct connection mode; externally managed/manual tunnels without `sshTarget` still do not start a managed SSH process; no config migration, schema rewrite, or new transport protocol is introduced.
- Risk labels considered: security-boundary, compatibility, availability, and process lifecycle.
- Risk explanation: The main risk is accidentally widening plaintext `ws://` allowance beyond managed SSH ownership.
- Why acceptable: The allowance is limited to config-derived SSH remotes with SSH tunnel ownership; after tunnel startup the runtime client/probe URL is loopback, and tests cover direct transport, default SSH transport, missing `sshTarget`, tunnel cleanup, status diagnostics, remote-port defaulting, gateway-status port splitting, logs routing, and SSH spawn failure behavior.

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes #26250, where configured SSH transport could be rejected by the remote plaintext `ws://` security check before the tunnel was established.
- Ensures configured SSH remote Gateway clients and probes start the managed tunnel first, then connect through the local forwarded endpoint.
- Keeps documented SSH defaults intact: omitted `gateway.remote.transport` behaves as SSH when `sshTarget` is present, and omitted `gateway.remote.remotePort` forwards to remote Gateway port `18789`.
- Makes status/diagnostic paths resilient when SSH tunnel startup fails, so users still get a status report with Gateway shown as unreachable.
- Keeps `openclaw gateway status` from forwarding the local tunnel listener port to the wrong remote Gateway port.

Why does this matter now?

Issue #26250 reports that the SSH transport security check rejects the connection before the SSH tunnel can be established. That blocks the documented safe remote Gateway setup even though the plaintext `ws://` URL is intended to become a loopback tunnel endpoint before client connection.

What is the intended outcome?

Configured SSH remote users can keep `gateway.remote.url` as the configured Gateway endpoint and `gateway.remote.sshTarget` as the SSH host. OpenClaw starts the tunnel, rewrites the runtime connection to the local forwarded endpoint, uses the documented remote port default, and cleans up the tunnel when the owning client/probe path finishes or fails.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This does not add a new transport protocol, change CLI/env URL override safety rules, make `transport: "direct"` bypass plaintext protection, or require a config migration. The scope is intentionally limited to configured Gateway SSH transport ownership and the surfaces that either start, diagnose, or retain that tunnel. It does not change provider routing, session persistence, message delivery semantics, auth policy, schemas, migrations, UI, or external manually managed tunnel behavior.

What does success look like?

A config-derived SSH remote starts a managed tunnel before client connection, default SSH transport works when `transport` is omitted, the remote side defaults to port `18789` when `remotePort` is omitted, direct transport remains explicit, status/logs paths share the same tunnel-aware contract, and diagnostic commands do not crash when SSH tunnel startup fails.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please focus on the ownership boundary for managed SSH tunnels: plaintext allowance is limited to config-derived SSH remotes, the runtime connection is rewritten to loopback immediately after tunnel startup, and tunnel cleanup remains tied to the owning Gateway client/probe path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #26250

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #26250

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request; this follows the public issue report.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Configured SSH remote Gateway URLs are no longer rejected before the managed tunnel path can run; tunnel setup rewrites the runtime URL to loopback; status diagnostics survive tunnel startup failure; default SSH transport and default remote-side port are preserved; `gateway status` no longer reuses the local listener port as the remote SSH destination port.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw CLI process, real foreground OpenClaw Gateway process, and an isolated local OpenSSH daemon using strict host-key checking, public-key auth, token auth, and `ssh -L` direct-tcpip forwarding. The configured remote URL used a non-loopback TEST-NET-3 host so the proof exercises the same plaintext guard boundary as the issue report without relying on an external server.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Start an isolated OpenSSH daemon on loopback with a generated host key and generated client identity.
  - Start a real foreground OpenClaw Gateway on a separate loopback port with token auth enabled.
  - Configure the client profile with `gateway.mode: "remote"`, `gateway.remote.url: "ws://203.0.113.10:<localTunnelPort>"`, `gateway.remote.transport: "ssh"`, `gateway.remote.sshTarget`, `gateway.remote.sshIdentity`, `gateway.remote.remotePort`, and `gateway.remote.token`.
  - Run `openclaw gateway call health --json` through that config-derived SSH remote.
  - Run `openclaw gateway call health --url ws://203.0.113.10:<localTunnelPort> --token <token> --json` as the explicit override safety probe.
  - Run focused regression/type/diff checks listed in the validation section below.
- Evidence after fix (copied terminal output):

  ```text
  Configured remote URL: ws://203.0.113.10:34829
  SSH target: 127.0.0.1:42069
  Remote Gateway port: 35601
  Local tunnel preferred port: 34829
  configured SSH gateway call exit: 0
  configured SSH gateway status exit: 0
  explicit --url override exit: 1
  sshd accepted publickey: True
  sshd observed direct-tcpip forwarding to Gateway: True
  leftover OpenClaw ssh forward process: none
  ```

  ```text
  Configured SSH health response:
  ok: true
  JSON keys included: agents, channelLabels, channelOrder, channels, defaultAgentId, durationMs, eventLoop, heartbeatSeconds, modelPricing, ok, plugins, sessions, ts
  ```

  ```text
  Explicit --url override safety probe:
  Gateway call failed: Error: SECURITY ERROR: Gateway URL "ws://203.0.113.10:34829" uses plaintext ws:// to a non-loopback address.
  Both credentials and chat data would be exposed to network interception.
  Source: cli --url
  Fix: Use wss:// for remote gateway URLs.
  ```

  ```text
   RUN  v4.1.8 [local path redacted]

   Test Files  6 passed (6)
        Tests  369 passed (369)
     Duration  7.15s
  ```

  ```text
   RUN  v4.1.8 [local path redacted]

   Test Files  4 passed (4)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  6.68s
  ```

  ```text
   RUN  v4.1.8 [local path redacted]

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  7 passed (7)
     Duration  1.06s
  ```

  ```text
  Validation result: pass, exit_code=0, 15479ms
  Validation result: pass, exit_code=0, 9527ms
  Validation result: pass, exit_code=0, 112ms
  ```

  ```text
  Focused sibling Gateway surface validation after local review:
  TUI Gateway chat: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 28 passed
  Gateway-backed TTS conversion: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 100 passed
  Security audit Gateway probe: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 2 passed
  Doctor health Gateway diagnostics: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 49 passed
  Devices local fallback check: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 44 passed
  Crestodian overview Gateway probe: Test Files 1 passed; Tests 2 passed
  ```

- Observed result after fix: The config-derived SSH remote opened an SSH session, sshd recorded public-key authentication and direct-tcpip forwarding to the real Gateway port, `openclaw gateway call health --json` returned `ok: true` through the configured SSH remote, and the managed tunnel process was gone after the CLI call completed. The explicit `--url` override with the same plaintext non-loopback URL still failed the plaintext safety check instead of receiving the configured-SSH exception.
- What was not tested: A public external SSH host was not used; the live proof used an isolated local OpenSSH daemon to avoid external credentials and network dependency while still exercising the real `ssh` binary, host-key checking, port forwarding, OpenClaw Gateway, and OpenClaw CLI.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The live proof runs the SSH server and Gateway on local loopback with generated keys/tokens, so it validates the transport lifecycle and safety boundary without proving internet routing, remote host firewalls, or a production SSH account configuration.
- Before evidence: Regression tests first failed because configured SSH `ws://` URLs hit plaintext validation before tunnel setup, omitted `remotePort` forwarded to the local URL port instead of `18789`, gateway-status forwarded local `19089` to remote `19089`, and SSH spawn errors surfaced as unhandled child-process errors rather than tunnel startup failures.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/ssh-transport.test.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-client.config.ts src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/status.scan.shared.test.ts src/commands/health.test.ts src/commands/doctor-gateway-health.test.ts src/commands/gateway-status.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/ssh-tunnel.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts src/tui/gateway-chat.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts src/cli/capability-cli.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-security.config.ts src/security/audit-gateway-ssh-transport.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-src.config.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/crestodian/overview.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Configured SSH transport opens a tunnel and rewrites the Gateway URL to loopback before client connection.
- Omitted `gateway.remote.transport` behaves as the documented default SSH transport when `sshTarget` is configured.
- Plaintext remote Gateway URLs are allowed only for callers that own the configured SSH tunnel.
- `openclaw logs` configured SSH remotes fall through to the normal Gateway RPC path and preserve tunnel-aware connection details when a transport error is reported.
- TUI Gateway chat starts the configured SSH tunnel before returning a remote Gateway URL, and stops it if remote auth resolution fails or Gateway client construction throws before ownership transfers to the chat client.
- Gateway-backed TTS conversion reads configured SSH Gateway details without tripping the pre-tunnel plaintext guard.
- Security audit and Crestodian overview Gateway probes start the configured SSH tunnel, probe the loopback URL, and stop the tunnel after probing.
- Security audit deep probes convert configured SSH tunnel startup failures into a failed Gateway probe warning instead of aborting the whole audit report.
- Doctor health auth-profile diagnostics can summarize configured SSH Gateway details without connecting or starting a tunnel.
- Devices local fallback checks do not replace Gateway pairing errors while inspecting configured SSH remotes.
- Call/bootstrap/status/health/doctor/ACP/MCP/operator approval paths clean up owned SSH tunnels on startup/connect failures.
- Status scans convert SSH tunnel startup failures into an unreachable Gateway snapshot instead of aborting the whole status report.
- Omitted `gateway.remote.remotePort` forwards to remote Gateway port `18789`, while the config URL port remains the preferred local listener.
- `openclaw gateway status` passes separate local listener and remote destination ports to the SSH tunnel.
- SSH spawn failures reject through `startSshPortForward` instead of becoming unhandled child-process errors.
- POSIX SSH tunnel startup uses the trusted `/usr/bin/ssh` launcher instead of inherited `PATH` lookup; Windows keeps platform executable resolution.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The issue reproduction path failed because the configured SSH remote URL was rejected by plaintext validation before tunnel setup.
- The default SSH transport regression test failed because `startSshPortForward` was not called when `remote.transport` was omitted.
- The logs configured-SSH regression test failed because the logs path rebuilt connection details without the configured SSH allowance.
- The sibling-surface regressions failed because TUI chat, gateway-backed TTS preflight, security audit, doctor health diagnostics, devices fallback checks, and Crestodian overview did not consistently apply the configured SSH allowance and tunnel ownership boundary.
- The TUI auth-failure cleanup regression failed because the SSH tunnel could be started before remote auth resolution threw, leaving no returned client owner to stop it.
- The TUI construction-failure cleanup regression failed because `GatewayChatClient.connect()` returned ownership only after constructing the Gateway client; a constructor throw could leave the managed SSH tunnel running.
- The status regression test failed because tunnel startup errors rejected the whole status snapshot.
- The security audit deep-probe regression failed because SSH tunnel startup errors aborted the audit before `gateway.probe_failed` could be reported.
- The remote-port regression test failed because the remote SSH side inherited the local tunnel URL port.
- The gateway-status regression test failed because the status SSH probe used local `19089` as remote `19089` instead of remote `18789`.
- The SSH spawn regression test failed with an unhandled child-process error instead of a promise rejection.
- The POSIX SSH launcher regression failed because tunnel startup spawned bare `ssh`, allowing inherited `PATH` lookup instead of the trusted system SSH binary.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests were added or updated for the SSH transport default behavior, plaintext allowance scope, Gateway bootstrap/call paths, logs CLI behavior, tunnel cleanup paths, status diagnostics, gateway-status port splitting, remote port defaulting, and SSH spawn error handling.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Configured SSH remote Gateway users can now connect through the managed tunnel instead of being rejected before tunnel startup, and status/diagnostics handle tunnel startup failures more gracefully.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. The documented defaults for `gateway.remote.transport` and `gateway.remote.remotePort` are now honored in the managed SSH paths; no migration is required.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. The network path changes for configured SSH remotes, but the plaintext allowance is scoped to config-derived SSH remotes with tunnel ownership, and the final runtime target is loopback.

What is the highest-risk area?

Accidentally widening plaintext `ws://` allowance beyond the managed SSH tunnel ownership path.

How is that risk mitigated?

CLI/env URL overrides and explicit direct transport still use the existing plaintext safety check. Only config-derived remote URLs with configured SSH transport and an SSH target receive the allowance, and tests cover default SSH, direct transport, missing `sshTarget`, tunnel cleanup, status diagnostic failure, logs routing, gateway-status local/remote port splitting, remote-port defaulting, and SSH spawn failure behavior.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after branch submission and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Author-side local validation and live configured-SSH Gateway proof are complete. The latest local ClawSweeper commit review found no actionable code finding; its remaining uncertainty was proof visibility in the local review context, which this PR body addresses with copied live proof and focused validation output. CI will rerun after branch submission.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

No maintainer review comments yet. ClawSweeper review findings are mapped explicitly:

- RF-001 / RF-002: Fixed by adding redacted terminal output from a live configured-SSH Gateway run through a real OpenClaw CLI process, real foreground OpenClaw Gateway process, and isolated local OpenSSH daemon.
- RF-003: Disclosed and covered by compatibility guardrails: omitted `gateway.remote.transport` follows the documented SSH default only when an SSH target is configured, and omitted `gateway.remote.remotePort` keeps the documented remote-side default `18789`.
- RF-004: Fixed/mitigated by the live proof showing the config-derived SSH remote reaches Gateway through managed SSH forwarding, while an explicit `--url ws://203.0.113.10:<localTunnelPort>` override still fails the plaintext security guard with `Source: cli --url`.
- RF-005: Disclosed as the remaining maintainer judgement area after contributor proof: compatibility/security-boundary acceptance, not an additional narrow code repair.

Local ClawSweeper review identified status resilience, remote-port defaulting, SSH spawn-error handling, gateway-status port-contract gaps, sibling Gateway surfaces that still needed configured-SSH allowance or tunnel ownership, TUI auth-failure tunnel cleanup before ownership transfer, a Crestodian SSH tunnel test-double typecheck mismatch, and security audit deep-probe behavior for SSH tunnel startup failures; each was fixed with focused regression or typecheck coverage.
target.author: zhangguiping-xydt

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
